perturbation.original_text: The Consultant will be reimbursed by the Company for all reasonable business expenses incurred by the Consultant in connection with his duties. This includes, but is not limited to, payments of expenses incurred when traveling abroad and others. In this connection, the Consultant will be issued, as soon as practicable, a Company credit card that the Consultant will use to pay for any and all expenses that pertain to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Consultant will be reimbursed by the Company for business expenses, to the extent the Company deems appropriate. Reimbursement will be determined by the Board of Directors' discretion. A Company credit card may be issued at the Board's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'all reasonable business expenses' to expenses 'the Company deems appropriate' and making the issuance of a credit card discretionary, the contract now contradicts standard business practice laws requiring reimbursement for legitimately incurred expenses.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification weakens the consultant's right to reimbursement, making it subject to the company's discretion. While this specific section doesn't directly mandate expense reimbursement, the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), particularly Section 207 regarding overtime, implicitly assumes that employees (and by extension, consultants whose compensation is analogous to wages) will be reimbursed for expenses incurred on behalf of the employer. By making reimbursement discretionary, the contract creates a situation where the consultant may not be adequately compensated for their time and effort, effectively reducing their rate of pay below minimum wage or impacting overtime calculations if the consultant is misclassified as an independent contractor, violating the spirit and intent of the FLSA.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language stated that the consultant would be reimbursed for "all reasonable business expenses", while the changed text makes reimbursement subject to the Company's discretion. The Law Explanation suggests that by making reimbursement discretionary, the contract creates a situation where the consultant may not be adequately compensated, violating the intent of the FLSA. While the provided scraped snippet is empty, the modification undermines the consultant's right to reimbursement, potentially contradicting fair compensation standards.

perturbation.original_text: For the purpose of this Article 3, "Cause" means: (i) Breach of Agreement. Consultant's material breach of Consultant's obligations of this Agreement, not cured after 30 days' Notice from the Company. 5 (ii) Gross Negligence. Consultant's gross negligence in the performance of Consultant's duties. (iii) Crimes and Dishonesty. Consultant's conviction of or plea guilty to any crime involving, dishonesty, fraud or moral turpitude.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purpose of this Article 3, "Cause" means: (i) Breach of Agreement. Consultant's breach of any obligation of this Agreement, not cured after 5 days' Notice from the Company. 5 (ii) Negligence. Consultant's negligence in the performance of Consultant's duties. (iii) Crimes and Dishonesty. Consultant's accusation for any crime involving, dishonesty, fraud or moral turpitude.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a breach of contract from 30 days to 5 days and changing 'gross negligence' to 'negligence' makes it easier to terminate the contract for cause. Also, changing 'conviction of or plea guilty' to 'accusation' changes the standard of evidence, and does not protect consultant from false accusations.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Labor Law § 195 outlines employer responsibilities regarding wage payment and notification. While not directly addressing termination clauses, the modifications affecting 'Cause' can indirectly violate this law. By lowering the bar for termination 'For Cause', the employer could potentially withhold earned wages or benefits based on a premature or unjust termination, resulting in an unlawful deduction or failure to pay earned compensation as required by NY Labor Law. The reduced cure period and lowered standard of proof for negligence increase the risk of wrongful termination and wage disputes, conflicting with the law's intent to protect employees' earned compensation.

(LAB) CHAPTER 31, ARTICLE 6 § 195. Notice and record-keeping requirements. Every employer shall: 1. (a) provide his or her employees, in writing in English and in the language identified by each employee as the primary language of such employee, at the time of hiring, a notice containing the following information: the rate or rates of pay and basis thereof, whether paid by the hour, shift, day, week, salary, piece, commission, or other; allowances, if any, claimed as part of the minimum wage, including tip, meal, or lodging allowances; the benefit portion of the minimum rate of home care aide total compensation as defined in section thirty-six hundred fourteen-c of the public health law ("home care aide benefits"), if applicable; prevailing wage supplements, if any, claimed as part of any prevailing wage or similar requirement pursuant to article eight of this chapter; the regular pay day designated by the employer in accordance with section one hundred ninety-one of this article; the name of the employer; any "doing business as" names used by the employer; the physical address of the employer's main office or principal place of business, and a mailing address if different; the telephone number of the employer; plus such other information as the commissioner deems material and necessary. Where such prevailing wage supplements are claimed, or such home care aide benefits are provided, the notice shall identify, for each type of supplement claimed or each type of home care aide benefits provided: (i) the hourly rate claimed; (ii) the type of supplement or type of home care aide benefits, including when applicable, but not limited to, pension or healthcare; (iii) the names and addresses of the person or entity providing such supplement or such home care aide benefits; and (iv) the agreement, if any, requiring or providing for such supplement or such home care aide benefits, together with information on how copies of such ag
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction by significantly weakening the protections afforded to the consultant, potentially leading to wage disputes and wrongful termination, as explained by the LLM. The original contract provided a 30-day cure period for breaches and required 'gross negligence' for termination based on negligence. It also required conviction or a guilty plea for crimes and dishonesty. The changed contract reduces the cure period to 5 days, changes the standard to 'negligence,' and allows termination based on 'accusation.' This could result in termination without due process and withholding of earned wages, which would violate the spirit of New York Labor Law § 195, as the LLM suggested. Scraped law snippet 1 refers to section 195 which concerns notice and record-keeping requirements for employers which is connected to the LLM explanation. The lowering of standards for termination creates a direct contradiction because it may impede legally required wage payments. The change from 'conviction of or plea guilty' to 'accusation' creates a situation where the consultant might be terminated for unproven allegations. This constitutes a contradiction because wages could be withheld based on accusation rather than legal finding, conflicting with the goal of ensuring employees receive earned compensation.

perturbation.original_text: The sole and exclusive place of jurisdiction in any matter arising out of or in connection with this Agreement will be the applicable New York state or federal court.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company alone decides on the place of jurisdiction in any matter arising out of or in connection with this Agreement, which could be New York.
perturbation.explanation: The changed text allows the Company to unilaterally choose the jurisdiction. This creates an imbalance of power and could lead to unfair forum selection.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 addresses the change of venue in federal courts for the convenience of parties and witnesses, and in the interest of justice. While parties can agree to a forum selection clause, granting one party the unilateral right to choose the jurisdiction after a dispute arises undermines the principles of fairness and convenience enshrined in this statute. This modification could force the consultant to litigate in an inconvenient or biased forum, contradicting the spirit of § 1404.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the principle of fairness in venue selection, potentially undermining the convenience of parties and witnesses. 28 U.S. Code § 1404 aims to ensure fairness by allowing a court to transfer a case for the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, as seen in both provided snippets. The original text ensures a neutral selection (NY state or federal court). By granting the company the sole decision on jurisdiction, the changed text allows the company to choose a location that may be inconvenient or biased against the other party, directly contradicting the spirit of § 1404 and its goal of ensuring convenience and justice.
